skill.name: follow-up
skill.description: Create concise follow-up emails for sales, partnerships, recruiting, and client outreach. The skill adapts tone and structure by follow-up number.
skill.summary: Draft short follow-up emails that add value and keep outreach respectful.
skill.author: humanizerai

security_audit.summary: The static external command finding is a false positive because SKILL.md lines 76-82 contain a fenced Markdown email output template, not executable Ruby or shell code. The weak cryptographic algorithm finding at SKILL.md line 3 is also a false positive; no cryptographic operation appears in the reviewed skill text.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Output Template
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged SKILL.md lines 76-82 as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The lines are a fenced Markdown block showing the expected email output format, with no executable command or user input execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is inside a Markdown code fence and only contains email template placeholders. No shell command, Ruby interpolation, or execution instruction is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged SKILL.md line 3 for a weak cryptographic algorithm. The line is front matter description text about follow-up emails, and no hash, cipher, encryption, or cryptographic API is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected line contains only natural language metadata. There is no evidence of MD5, DES, SHA-1, or any related cryptographic implementation.

content.value_statement: Unanswered outreach often needs a respectful reminder with a fresh reason to reply. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code draft short follow-ups by sequence stage.
actual_capabilities: Parses original outreach context, timing, follow-up number, and optional new context.
Selects a bump, value-add, or breakup framework based on the follow-up stage.
Drafts concise email copy with one clear call to action.
Avoids guilt-based language, empty check-ins, and repeated original copy.
Reports the framework used, what changed, and the word count.
limitations: Requires enough original outreach context to produce a specific message.
Does not verify recipient data, inbox status, or CRM history.
Does not send emails or integrate with mail platforms.
May need user review for regulated, legal, or sensitive communications.

use_cases.title: Sales Prospect Follow-Up
use_cases.description: Write a short second touch that adds value without repeating the original pitch.

use_cases.title: Partnership Outreach
use_cases.description: Reopen a conversation with a new angle, shared context, or relevant resource.

use_cases.title: Recruiting Follow-Up
use_cases.description: Send a polite reminder to a candidate or hiring contact after no response.

prompt_templates.title: Simple First Follow-Up
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a first reminder after initial outreach.
prompt_templates.prompt: Write follow-up number 1. Original email context: [paste context]. It was sent [number] days ago. Keep it brief and friendly.

prompt_templates.title: Value-Add Follow-Up
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for a second message with a new reason to reply.
prompt_templates.prompt: Write follow-up number 2 for this outreach: [paste context]. Add this new value: [resource, insight, or trigger]. Use one clear call to action.

prompt_templates.title: Breakup Follow-Up
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for a final respectful close.
prompt_templates.prompt: Write follow-up number 3 for this prospect: [paste context]. Make it a permission-based close, give an easy out, and avoid pressure.

prompt_templates.title: Sequence Optimization
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for improving a multi-step outreach sequence.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this original email and planned follow-ups: [paste sequence]. Rewrite the next follow-up, explain the framework, and show what changed.

output_examples.input: A sales rep followed up three days after sending a demo invitation to a marketing leader.
output_examples.output: A short reply-thread email that references the demo idea, adds a useful campaign insight, and asks if a quick review next week is useful.

output_examples.input: A founder needs a second follow-up to an investor after sharing a brief company update.
output_examples.output: A concise value-add follow-up with one new traction point, a simple meeting request, and a note explaining the selected framework.

output_examples.input: A recruiter needs a final message after two unanswered candidate emails.
output_examples.output: A respectful closing email that gives the candidate an easy way to pause the conversation or suggest a better time.

best_practices: Provide the original email, elapsed time, and exact follow-up number.
Add one new detail, resource, or trigger event when possible.
Review the draft for recipient accuracy before sending.
anti_patterns: Do not resend the same message with only a new opening line.
Do not ask for several actions in one follow-up.
Do not use guilt, pressure, or vague check-in language.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates concise follow-up email drafts and explains the framework used.

faq.question: Which follow-up numbers does it support?
faq.answer: It supports first, second, and third follow-ups with different strategies for each stage.

faq.question: Can it write a final breakup email?
faq.answer: Yes. It can write a respectful final follow-up that gives the recipient an easy out.

faq.question: Does it send emails?
faq.answer: No. It only drafts text for review and use in another email tool.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the original email context, days since sending, follow-up number, and any new context.

faq.question: Is it suitable for sensitive outreach?
faq.answer: Use extra review for legal, medical, financial, or employment-sensitive messages.
